headline: Behavioral training and tool discipline displaced raw scale as the quarter's defining efficiency theme, while model compression and recursive self-improvement cooled.
body: The quarter's clearest rotation is toward small models trained to behave: Snorkel showed a 4B model finetuned with reinforcement learning and tool discipline outperforming Qwen 3 235B on structured query tasks, self-correcting where the larger model hallucinated [2], and Gemma 4's 31B variant ranked fourth among open models at 2-20x smaller size, running on single GPUs and phones under Apache 2.0 [5]. Against that, model compression, AI system architecture, and recursive self-improvement all registered as weakening despite high ratios, and skills, MCP, LSP, subagents, and token optimization lagged. Breakouts clustered around agent plumbing: workflow automation, memory systems, and attribution, reflected in PAI's persistent-memory Life OS on Claude Code [4] and PostHog's signal-to-pull-request pipeline [7]. New entrants percolation, database scaling, GPU acceleration, and Untied Ulysses surfaced without anchoring items, alongside security and verification failures in AI worms, prompt injection, and fabricated legal citations [9][10].

cited_items.title: Stop Making Models Bigger, Make Them Behave — Kobie Crawdord, Snorkel
Sovereign Escape Velocity: Ownership w Open Models — Gus Martins, & Ian Ballantyne, Google DeepMind
PAI: The Life OS for AI-Powered Development
Self Driving Products: Product Signals to Pull Requests — Joshua Snyder, PostHog
Can you social engineer an AI? Plus: AI worms and the nonhuman identity problem
Lawyers Barred for A.I.-Generated Citations to Fake Cases - The New York Times
